title: Announcement: Campus Career Fair
transcript:
Good morning, students. This is an important announcement about the upcoming Fall Career Fair. The fair will take place next Wednesday, October 14th, in the main gymnasium from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. This is one of the largest events of the semester, and this year more than eighty companies will be attending, representing fields from engineering and healthcare to business and the arts.

Here are a few things to keep in mind so you can make the most of the day. First, dress professionally. First impressions matter, and recruiters do notice. Second, bring several printed copies of your resume. Even though many companies accept online applications, having a physical copy ready to hand over makes a strong impression. If you need help polishing your resume, the Career Center is offering free walk-in reviews all this week.

Third, do a little research beforehand. A full list of attending companies is posted on the Career Center website. We strongly recommend choosing a few you're most interested in and learning about them in advance, so you can ask thoughtful questions.

Finally, don't forget to bring your student ID, which is required for entry. We look forward to seeing you there, and we wish you great success in your job search.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of this announcement?
options: To cancel the Fall Career Fair
To inform students about the career fair and how to prepare
To advertise a resume-writing course for credit
To announce a change in gym hours
questions.explanation: The announcement informs students about the upcoming career fair and gives advice on how to prepare, which is its main purpose.

questions.q: Why does the announcement advise bringing printed copies of a resume?
options: Because online applications are not allowed
Because a physical copy makes a strong impression
Because recruiters cannot read digital files
Because printing is not available at the fair
questions.explanation: The announcement says that even though companies accept online applications, having a physical copy ready makes a strong impression.

questions.q: Where can students find a list of attending companies?
options: On the main gymnasium door
On the Career Center website
In the student newspaper
At the entrance on the day of the fair
questions.explanation: The announcement states that a full list of attending companies is posted on the Career Center website.

questions.q: What is required for entry to the career fair?
options: A printed resume
A student ID
An appointment with the Career Center
A registration fee
questions.explanation: The announcement notes that a student ID is required for entry to the fair.

title: Announcement: Gym Closure for Renovation
transcript:
Attention, members of the University Recreation Center. We want to let you know about some important changes coming up next month. Starting Monday, November 2nd, the main fitness center on the first floor will be closed for renovations. The upgrade will include new cardio equipment, additional weight machines, and improved lighting and ventilation. We expect the work to take about three weeks, with a planned reopening on Monday, November 23rd.

We know this is a busy time of year, and we don't want to leave you without options. During the renovation, the second-floor studio will remain open and will host an expanded schedule of group fitness classes, including yoga, cycling, and strength training. In addition, members can use the swimming pool and the indoor track, both of which will keep their normal hours.

We've also arranged something special. During the closure, your membership card will grant you free access to the Downtown Community Gym, located just two blocks from campus. Simply show your university ID and membership card at their front desk.

We appreciate your patience during this upgrade, and we're confident you'll love the improved facilities when they reopen. For questions, please stop by the front desk or check our website.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of this announcement?
options: To announce that the recreation center is closing permanently
To inform members about a temporary gym renovation and alternatives
To advertise new membership rates
To cancel all group fitness classes
questions.explanation: The announcement informs members that the main fitness center will close for renovation and describes alternative options, which is its purpose.

questions.q: What improvements will the renovation include?
options: A new swimming pool and indoor track
New cardio equipment, weight machines, and better lighting and ventilation
A larger parking lot
A new second-floor studio
questions.explanation: The announcement lists new cardio equipment, additional weight machines, and improved lighting and ventilation as part of the upgrade.

questions.q: What can members do at the Downtown Community Gym during the closure?
options: Get free access by showing their university ID and membership card
Sign up for a paid membership at a discount
Take classes not offered on campus
Store their gym equipment
questions.explanation: The announcement says members can get free access to the Downtown Community Gym by showing their university ID and membership card.

questions.q: What can be inferred about facilities like the pool and indoor track during the renovation?
options: They will be closed along with the fitness center.
They will remain open at their normal hours.
They will move to the second-floor studio.
They will only be open on weekends.
questions.explanation: The announcement states that the swimming pool and indoor track will keep their normal hours during the renovation.

title: Chemistry lecture room change
transcript: Good morning, everyone. Before Professor Hensley begins, I have a quick announcement about next week's schedule. Starting Monday, our Introduction to Chemistry lectures will no longer meet here in Whitmore Hall, Room 210. The building's ventilation system is being upgraded, and the work is expected to last through the end of the month. So for the next three weeks, all lectures will move across campus to the Science Center, Room 145. That's the large auditorium on the ground floor, just past the main entrance. Please note that the lecture time stays exactly the same, nine fifteen on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. Only the location is changing. I'd also remind you that the Friday discussion sections are not affected; those still meet in their usual seminar rooms in Whitmore. If you're unsure which room your section uses, check the course website. And please arrive a few minutes early on Monday, since the new auditorium can be a little tricky to find. Thanks.

questions.q: Why does the speaker make this announcement?
options: To explain a change in the location of a course's lectures
To introduce a new professor for the chemistry course
To cancel all classes while a building is repaired
To announce a change in the time of the lectures
questions.explanation: The speaker's main point is that lectures are relocating from Whitmore Hall 210 to the Science Center auditorium, while the meeting time stays the same.

questions.q: What is the reason for the change?
options: The current room is too small for the class
The building's ventilation system is being upgraded
The professor requested a larger auditorium
Another course needs to use Room 210
questions.explanation: The speaker says the move is necessary because the building's ventilation system is being upgraded through the end of the month.

questions.q: What does the speaker suggest students do on Monday?
options: Meet first in their Friday discussion sections
Contact the professor about their section room
Arrive early because the new room is hard to find
Confirm that the Friday lectures were canceled
questions.explanation: The speaker advises arriving a few minutes early because the new auditorium can be a little tricky to find.

title: Library study room reservation policy
transcript: Attention, library users. We'd like to let you know about a change to our group study room policy that takes effect this Saturday. Until now, students could reserve a study room for up to four hours at a time. Because demand has grown so much this semester, we're reducing the maximum reservation to two hours per group. This will let more students get access to the rooms, especially during busy exam periods. You can still book online through the library portal, and reservations open seventy-two hours in advance. One more thing: if your group hasn't arrived within fifteen minutes of your start time, the system will automatically release the room to the next person in line. So please be punctual, or cancel ahead of time if your plans change. We know this is a big adjustment, but we hope it makes the space fairer for everyone. If you have questions, staff at the front desk are always happy to help. Thank you.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To explain how to book a study room online
To describe the library's new exam-period hours
To introduce an updated library booking portal
To announce a new limit on study room reservations
questions.explanation: The announcement introduces a new rule cutting the maximum study-room reservation from four hours to two.

questions.q: What happens if a group is more than fifteen minutes late?
options: The group is charged a small fee
The reservation is extended automatically
The room is released to the next person in line
Staff call the group to remind them
questions.explanation: If a group has not arrived within fifteen minutes, the system automatically releases the room to the next person in line.

questions.q: What does the speaker imply about the study rooms?
options: Too few students have been using them
They are in very high demand this semester
They need repairs before the exam period
They will be closed during busy periods
questions.explanation: The change is meant to give more students access, especially during exams, which implies the rooms are in high demand.

title: Fall career fair reminder
transcript: Hi, everyone. I want to remind you all about the fall career fair, which is coming up next Thursday in the Student Union ballroom. It runs from ten in the morning until four in the afternoon, and more than eighty employers have confirmed they'll be there, everything from tech startups to hospitals to government agencies. This is a great chance to hand out your resume, practice talking with recruiters, and maybe even line up an interview. A couple of tips. First, dress professionally; a lot of students underestimate how much of a difference that makes. Second, bring plenty of printed copies of your resume, at least fifteen or twenty. And third, do a little homework beforehand. The full list of companies is posted on the Career Center website, so look it over and pick a few you're excited about. That way you can ask smart questions and stand out. Oh, and admission is free, but you'll need your student ID to get in. See you there.

questions.q: Why is the speaker giving this announcement?
options: To remind students about an upcoming career fair
To teach students how to write a better resume
To describe new companies now hiring on campus
To explain how to schedule a job interview
questions.explanation: The speaker is reminding students about the upcoming fall career fair and how to prepare for it.

questions.q: What does the speaker recommend students bring?
options: A written list of questions for each employer
A laptop for filling out applications
A letter of recommendation from a professor
At least fifteen to twenty copies of their resume
questions.explanation: The speaker advises bringing plenty of printed resumes, at least fifteen or twenty copies.

questions.q: What should students do before attending the fair?
options: Register for the event in advance online
Review the list of companies on the website
Email the employers they want to meet
Sign up for a resume-writing workshop
questions.explanation: Students are told to look over the company list on the Career Center website so they can ask smart questions and stand out.

title: Annual emergency evacuation drill
transcript: May I have your attention, please. This is a reminder that the university will hold its annual emergency evacuation drill tomorrow morning at ten o'clock. When you hear the alarm, a series of loud, repeating tones, please stop what you're doing and leave the building calmly through the nearest marked exit. Do not use the elevators; take the stairs instead. Once outside, move to the designated assembly area, which for this building is the grassy courtyard near the west parking lot. Please stay there until a fire marshal tells you it's safe to return. The whole drill should take no more than fifteen minutes. I want to stress that although this is only a practice, we ask everyone to treat it as though it were a real emergency. That's the best way to make sure we're all prepared. Instructors, please help direct any students who may be visiting the building for the first time. Thank you for your cooperation, and let's make this a smooth, quick drill.

questions.q: What is the announcement mainly about?
options: A recent emergency that occurred on campus
New locations for the building's exits
A scheduled emergency evacuation drill
Repairs being made to the alarm system
questions.explanation: The announcement is a reminder about tomorrow's scheduled annual emergency evacuation drill.

questions.q: Where should people go after leaving the building?
options: The nearest available classroom
The main lobby of the building
Anywhere inside the west parking lot
The grassy courtyard near the west parking lot
questions.explanation: The designated assembly area for this building is the grassy courtyard near the west parking lot.

questions.q: What does the speaker ask instructors to do?
options: Help direct students visiting the building for the first time
Take attendance before leaving the building
Test the alarm system before the drill begins
Use the elevators to assist slower students
questions.explanation: The speaker asks instructors to help direct any students who may be visiting the building for the first time.

title: Add/drop deadline reminder
transcript: Good afternoon. I want to make sure nobody misses an important deadline coming up. This Friday, the fifteenth, is the last day to add or drop a course for the spring semester without any penalty. After Friday, if you drop a class, it will show up on your transcript as a withdrawal, and you won't get a refund for it. So if you've been thinking about changing your schedule, now is the time to act. You can make all these changes yourself through the online registration system; you don't need to come to the office or fill out any paper forms. However, a few courses do require your advisor's approval before you can add them, and those tend to fill up quickly. If you're planning to add one of those, I'd strongly encourage you to email your advisor today rather than waiting until Friday afternoon. The system closes at exactly five o'clock, and it will not let you make changes after that, so don't leave it to the last minute. Thanks, everyone.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To explain how to use the online registration system
To remind students about the add/drop deadline
To describe changes to the spring course catalog
To announce new advising office hours
questions.explanation: The main purpose is to remind students that Friday is the last day to add or drop a course without penalty.

questions.q: What happens if a student drops a course after Friday?
options: It will appear as a withdrawal on their transcript
They will be automatically re-enrolled in it
They must get their advisor's written approval
The course is added back the following semester
questions.explanation: After Friday, dropping a class means it shows up on the transcript as a withdrawal, with no refund.

questions.q: What does the speaker suggest for adding a course that needs approval?
options: Waiting until Friday to make a final decision
Submitting a paper form to the registration office
Dropping another class first to make room
Emailing their advisor today rather than waiting
questions.explanation: For courses requiring approval, the speaker urges students to email their advisor today because those courses fill up quickly.

title: Scheduled campus Wi-Fi maintenance
transcript: Hello, this is an announcement from the Office of Information Technology. We want to give you advance notice that the campus wireless network will be down for scheduled maintenance this Sunday, from midnight until roughly six in the morning. During this window, you won't be able to connect to Wi-Fi anywhere on campus, including in the dorms, the library, and all academic buildings. The wired connections in faculty offices will not be affected. We chose these early-morning hours specifically to cause the least disruption, but we realize some of you like to study late or work on assignments overnight. If you have anything due Monday, we strongly recommend you download whatever files you'll need ahead of time, or plan to work somewhere off campus during those six hours. The upgrade will make the network noticeably faster once it's complete, so we appreciate your patience. If the network is still giving you trouble after six on Sunday, please contact the help desk and we'll look into it. Thank you.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To announce that a faster network is available now
To explain how to connect to the campus Wi-Fi
To notify people about scheduled Wi-Fi maintenance
To report an unexpected network outage
questions.explanation: The purpose is to give advance notice that scheduled maintenance will take the campus Wi-Fi offline early Sunday morning.

questions.q: What will continue to work during the maintenance window?
options: Wi-Fi in the residence halls
Wired connections in faculty offices
Wi-Fi in the library
The networks in academic buildings
questions.explanation: The speaker notes that the wired connections in faculty offices will not be affected.

questions.q: What does the speaker recommend for students with work due Monday?
options: Download the files they need in advance
Ask their instructors for an extension
Use the computers at the help desk office
Connect using a wired line in the dorms
questions.explanation: Students with Monday deadlines are advised to download needed files ahead of time or work off campus during the outage.

title: New student orientation schedule
transcript: Welcome, everyone, and congratulations again on joining the university. I'm going to walk you through how the rest of today's orientation will work. Right now we're all together in the main hall, but in about ten minutes we'll split into smaller groups by college, engineering, arts and sciences, business, and so on. Your group number is printed on the name tag you received at the front desk, so take a look at that now. Each group will spend the afternoon with a student guide who'll give you a walking tour of campus and show you the buildings you'll use most. Wear comfortable shoes, because there's a fair amount of walking involved. At four o'clock, everyone comes back here for a welcome reception with free food and a chance to meet some of the faculty. Attendance at the tour is not strictly required, but I really encourage you to go; it's the easiest way to learn your way around before classes start next week. Any questions can wait until we break into groups.

questions.q: What is the speaker mainly doing?
options: Introducing the members of the faculty
Giving a guided tour of the main hall
Handing out name tags to new students
Explaining how the orientation day is organized
questions.explanation: The speaker is walking students through how the rest of the orientation day will work, from splitting into groups to the reception.

questions.q: How can students find out which group they are in?
options: By checking their name tag
By asking their student guide
By looking at a list posted in the hall
By returning to the front desk
questions.explanation: The speaker says each student's group number is printed on the name tag they received at the front desk.

questions.q: What does the speaker imply about the walking tour?
options: It is required of all new students
It will be canceled if the weather is bad
It is optional but strongly recommended
It takes the place of the welcome reception
questions.explanation: The speaker says attendance is not strictly required but strongly encourages it, implying the tour is optional yet recommended.

title: New dining hall composting program
transcript: Hey, everyone, quick announcement from Dining Services. Starting next Monday, we're rolling out a new composting program in all three dining halls, and we need your help to make it work. You'll notice we've added a third bin next to the usual trash and recycling stations. The new green bin is for food scraps, things like fruit peels, leftover vegetables, and even napkins. Please don't put plastic, foil, or drink cups in there, because even a little bit of that can ruin an entire batch of compost. There will be clear signs above each bin with pictures, so when you're not sure, just check the picture. Student volunteers will also be standing by the bins during the first week to answer questions and point you to the right one. Our goal is to cut the amount of waste we send to the landfill roughly in half by the end of the year. It's a small change in your routine, but together it adds up to a big difference. Thanks for pitching in.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To explain why the dining halls will close
To introduce a new composting program in the dining halls
To describe a change to the dining hall menu
To recruit students to work in the kitchen
questions.explanation: The announcement introduces a new composting program launching in all three dining halls.

questions.q: What should be put in the new green bin?
options: Plastic cups and foil wrappers
Empty glass bottles
Food scraps such as fruit peels and napkins
Only clean, recyclable paper
questions.explanation: The green bin is for food scraps like fruit peels, leftover vegetables, and napkins, not plastic, foil, or cups.

questions.q: What does the speaker suggest doing when unsure which bin to use?
options: Ask a dining hall manager for help
Put the item in the regular trash
Wait until the program's second week
Check the pictures on the signs above the bins
questions.explanation: The speaker says there are clear signs with pictures above each bin, so when unsure, just check the picture.

title: Library Renovation and Temporary Study Space
transcript: Good morning, everyone. Before you head off to your study groups, I want to give you a heads-up about some changes happening here at the main library. Starting next Monday, the entire third floor will be closed for renovation. Now, I know a lot of you rely on those quiet study rooms up there, so let me tell you what we've arranged. For the duration of the project, which we expect to run about six weeks, we've set up an alternative study area in the basement of the Student Union, just across the courtyard. It's got the same long hours, open until two in the morning, and we've moved most of the comfortable chairs and about forty desks down there. One thing to keep in mind, though: the printers and scanners won't be relocated, so if you need to print something, you'll want to use the machines on the first floor of the library here, which stay open as usual. Also, the group study rooms in that temporary space can't be reserved online during the renovation. It'll be strictly first-come, first-served, so come early if you want a spot during exam week. We apologize for any inconvenience, but I promise the new third floor will be worth the wait. It'll have more outlets, better lighting, and soundproof booths. Thanks for your patience, and if you have questions, the front desk staff can help you out.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To explain how to reserve group study rooms online
To inform students about a library closure and temporary arrangements
To encourage students to join study groups this semester
To describe new printing equipment being installed
questions.explanation: The speaker's central message is the third-floor closure and the alternative study space; the other options are minor details or not mentioned.

questions.q: Where should students go if they need to print a document during the renovation?
options: The basement of the Student Union
The third floor of the library
The first floor of the library
The front desk of the Student Union
questions.explanation: The speaker states printers stay on the first floor of the library, not in the relocated study area or the closed third floor.

questions.q: Why does the speaker mention that the temporary rooms are first-come, first-served?
options: To warn students that online reservations won't work there
To explain that the rooms are free of charge
To announce that the rooms will close early
To suggest students study alone instead
questions.explanation: The point is made right after noting reservations can't be done online, so it clarifies the booking change; the other options are not implied.

questions.q: What can be inferred about the renovated third floor?
options: It will be smaller than the original space
It will offer improved facilities for studying
It will no longer include study rooms
It will be reserved only for graduate students
questions.explanation: The speaker lists more outlets, better lighting, and soundproof booths, implying improvements, while nothing suggests it will shrink or restrict access.

title: Campus Shuttle Schedule Change
transcript: Hi everyone, this is a quick service update from campus transportation. Beginning this Saturday, we're making some adjustments to the Route B shuttle, the one that runs between the north dorms and the science complex. Because of road construction on Maple Avenue, the shuttle will no longer stop at the corner of Maple and Fifth. Instead, we've added a temporary stop right in front of the recreation center, which is only about a two-minute walk away. If you usually catch the bus at the old stop, please plan for that small change. On top of that, we're actually increasing service during peak hours. From seven-thirty to nine-thirty in the morning, buses will now come every ten minutes instead of every fifteen, so your morning commute should be a little smoother. In the evenings, though, the last shuttle will now leave at eleven rather than midnight, so if you tend to stay late in the labs, keep that earlier cutoff in mind. All of these changes will be posted at the stops and on the transit app by Friday afternoon. And remember, the app shows real-time locations, so you can see exactly where your bus is instead of standing out in the cold guessing. If the construction wraps up ahead of schedule, we'll switch back to the regular route and let you know. Safe travels, and thanks for riding with us.

questions.q: Why is the Route B shuttle stop being moved?
options: Because of low ridership at the old stop
Because of road construction on Maple Avenue
Because the recreation center requested it
Because the science complex is closing
questions.explanation: The speaker directly attributes the change to construction on Maple Avenue; the other reasons are never given.

questions.q: What change is being made to evening service?
options: The last shuttle will leave earlier than before
Evening buses will run every ten minutes
Evening service is being canceled entirely
The last shuttle will leave at midnight
questions.explanation: The last shuttle moves from midnight to eleven, an earlier cutoff; the ten-minute frequency applies to mornings only.

questions.q: According to the speaker, what is one benefit of the transit app?
options: It offers discounted fares to students
It lets riders reserve a seat in advance
It shows the real-time location of buses
It provides free coffee at each stop
questions.explanation: The speaker highlights real-time locations so riders aren't left guessing; the other features are not mentioned.

questions.q: What will happen if the construction finishes early?
options: Route B will be permanently discontinued
The shuttle will return to its regular route
Service will be reduced to save money
A new stop will be added at the labs
questions.explanation: The speaker says they'll switch back to the regular route if construction wraps up ahead of schedule; the other outcomes contradict the talk.

title: Museum Tour Introduction
transcript: Welcome, everyone, to the City Natural History Museum. My name is Daniel, and I'll be your guide this afternoon. Before we start walking, let me give you a quick overview of what's ahead so you know what to expect. Our tour today will last about ninety minutes, and we'll focus mainly on the newly reopened marine life wing, which just finished a two-year restoration. Now, one thing that makes this wing special is the suspended whale skeleton in the central hall. It's nearly eighteen meters long, and it's the actual skeleton of a blue whale that washed ashore on our coast back in the nineteen-twenties. A team of scientists spent decades preserving it. As we move through the exhibits, I'd ask that you keep flash photography turned off. The lighting in some rooms is deliberately kept low to protect the more delicate specimens, and camera flashes can actually damage them over time. Regular photos without flash are perfectly fine, so snap away. At the end of the tour, we'll stop by the interactive touch tank, where you'll be able to gently handle a few live sea stars and other tide-pool creatures, so this is a great one for the kids. If at any point you can't hear me, just wave, and I'll speak up. Alright, if everyone's ready, let's begin over here by the entrance to the marine wing. Please stay together as a group.

questions.q: What is the speaker mainly doing in this talk?
options: Explaining the history of the entire museum
Introducing the tour and its guidelines
Describing how to become a museum volunteer
Comparing several different exhibits
questions.explanation: The guide is orienting visitors to the tour's plan and rules; he does not cover the museum's full history or volunteering.

questions.q: What is notable about the blue whale skeleton?
options: It was created as an artificial model
It is the smallest specimen in the wing
It comes from a whale that washed ashore in the 1920s
It was donated by another museum last year
questions.explanation: The guide says it is the real skeleton of a whale that washed ashore in the 1920s; it is neither a model nor a recent donation.

questions.q: Why does the speaker ask visitors to turn off flash photography?
options: Because flashes can damage delicate specimens
Because photography is banned in the museum
Because the flashes disturb other tour groups
Because the camera batteries drain too quickly
questions.explanation: The speaker explains that low lighting protects specimens and flashes can damage them over time; general photos are allowed.

questions.q: What will visitors be able to do at the end of the tour?
options: Purchase souvenirs at a gift shop
Watch a documentary film about whales
Touch live tide-pool creatures in a tank
Meet the scientists who restored the wing
questions.explanation: The guide mentions the interactive touch tank with live sea stars at the end; the other activities are not described.

title: New Student Orientation Talk
transcript: Hello, first-years, and welcome to campus. I'm one of the orientation leaders, and I want to spend a few minutes walking you through some things you'll need to take care of this week. First and most important: your student ID card. You can pick it up at the administration building anytime before Friday. That card isn't just for identification. It's how you get into the dining halls, it unlocks your dorm, and you'll tap it to borrow books, so don't put this off. Second, I strongly encourage you to attend at least one of the academic advising sessions. These are where you meet with a faculty advisor who helps you build your class schedule. Spots fill up fast, so sign up online today if you can. Now, a lot of you have been asking about the club fair. It's this Thursday afternoon on the main quad, and honestly, it's one of the best ways to meet people who share your interests, whether that's robotics, hiking, or debate. There will be over a hundred booths. One last practical tip: download the campus safety app before the weekend. It has a feature that lets you request a walking escort at night, which is really handy if you're heading back from the library late. That's it from me. Explore, ask questions, and don't be shy. Everyone here was new once, and we're all happy to help you find your way around.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To describe the university's academic history
To guide new students through important first-week tasks
To recruit students for the robotics club
To explain the rules of the dining halls
questions.explanation: The speaker walks first-years through several tasks to complete during their first week; the other options are narrow details.

questions.q: Why does the speaker say the student ID card is so important?
options: It gives access to dining halls, dorms, and library books
It is required to graduate on time
It replaces the need for a class schedule
It provides free public transportation
questions.explanation: The speaker lists dining, dorm access, and borrowing books as functions of the card; the other uses are not mentioned.

questions.q: What does the speaker recommend doing about academic advising?
options: Waiting until the second week to sign up
Meeting with other first-years instead of an advisor
Signing up online today because spots fill quickly
Skipping it unless you have a declared major
questions.explanation: The speaker urges signing up online today since spots fill fast; the other choices contradict the advice.

questions.q: What feature of the campus safety app does the speaker highlight?
options: It lets you order food to your dorm
It allows you to request a walking escort at night
It tracks your class attendance
It shows the club fair schedule
questions.explanation: The speaker points out the nighttime walking-escort feature; the other functions are invented distractors.

title: Recreation Center Pool Maintenance
transcript: Attention all members, this is a notice from the campus recreation center. We wanted to let you know in advance that the main swimming pool will be closed for annual maintenance from the fifteenth through the twenty-second of this month. During that week, our staff will be draining the pool, resurfacing the tiles, and giving the filtration system a thorough overhaul, which is something we do once a year to keep the water safe and clean. We know a lot of our regular swimmers count on their morning laps, so here's the good news. The smaller lap pool on the lower level will remain open the entire time. It's only four lanes instead of eight, so we're asking everyone to be courteous and share lanes during busy hours. Circle swimming will be in effect, meaning you stay to the right side of your lane, just like driving. If you've never done that before, the lifeguards will be happy to show you how. Also, please note that all group water aerobics classes are moved to the lower-level pool for that week, and a couple of the evening sessions have been shortened by fifteen minutes to fit the smaller space. The full adjusted class schedule is posted at the front desk and on our website. We appreciate your flexibility, and we promise the main pool will be sparkling and ready when it reopens on the twenty-third. Thanks, and keep swimming.

questions.q: What is the announcement mainly about?
options: A permanent closure of the recreation center
A temporary pool closure and alternative arrangements
A new membership pricing structure
The hiring of additional lifeguards
questions.explanation: The talk centers on the week-long maintenance closure and the backup lap pool; the other topics are not discussed.

questions.q: What does 'circle swimming' require members to do?
options: Swim only in the deep end of the pool
Swim in a clockwise loop around the whole pool
Stay to the right side of the lane while sharing it
Take turns using the lane one person at a time
questions.explanation: The speaker explains circle swimming means staying to the right of the lane like driving; the other definitions are incorrect.

questions.q: Why does the speaker mention that the main pool maintenance happens once a year?
options: To reassure members it is a routine safety measure
To warn that fees will increase annually
To explain why the pool is being permanently removed
To encourage members to volunteer for the work
questions.explanation: Framing it as annual upkeep to keep water safe reassures members it's routine; the other interpretations are unsupported.

questions.q: What change is being made to some evening aerobics classes?
options: They are canceled for the entire month
They are shortened by fifteen minutes
They are moved to the main pool
They are combined into a single class
questions.explanation: The speaker says a couple of evening sessions are shortened by fifteen minutes; they are relocated, not canceled or merged.

title: Career Fair Preparation Session
transcript: Good afternoon, everyone. Thanks for coming to this quick info session about next month's career fair. I want to make sure you all walk in prepared, because a little preparation really does make a difference. The fair takes place on the twelfth in the main gymnasium, and this year we've got a record number of employers signed up, more than eighty companies across engineering, finance, healthcare, and the creative fields. Here's my first piece of advice: do your homework before you go. Look up which companies are attending, and pick maybe five or six you're genuinely excited about. Trying to talk to all eighty is a recipe for exhaustion, and recruiters can tell when you haven't done any research. Second, bring plenty of printed copies of your resume. Yes, most applications happen online now, but handing a recruiter a physical copy still leaves a strong impression, and you never know when someone will ask for one. I'd bring at least fifteen. Third, and people forget this one, dress professionally even though it's on campus. First impressions form in seconds. Oh, and one logistical note: the career center is offering free professional headshots in the lobby that day, so bring a clean shirt if you want a nice photo for your online profiles. If you'd like a mock interview to practice beforehand, email our office and we'll pair you with a counselor. Alright, go get those offers.

questions.q: What is the speaker's main goal in this session?
options: To announce that the career fair has been canceled
To help students prepare for the upcoming career fair
To describe the history of the career center
To recruit students to work for a specific company
questions.explanation: The speaker gives practical advice for preparing for the fair; the other options misrepresent the talk's purpose.

questions.q: Why does the speaker suggest choosing only five or six companies?
options: Because only six companies are attending
Because talking to all of them is exhausting and unfocused
Because each student is limited to six interviews
Because the other companies charge a fee
questions.explanation: The speaker warns that targeting all eighty leads to exhaustion and shows lack of research; there is no attendance limit or fee.

questions.q: Why does the speaker recommend bringing printed resumes?
options: Because online applications are not accepted
Because printing is not available on campus
Because a physical copy still makes a strong impression
Because recruiters keep them as souvenirs
questions.explanation: Even with online applications, the speaker says a physical resume leaves a strong impression; the other reasons are not given.

questions.q: What free service is offered in the lobby on the day of the fair?
options: Professional headshots for online profiles
Free lunch for all attendees
Printed maps of the gymnasium
On-the-spot job offers
questions.explanation: The speaker mentions free professional headshots in the lobby; the other services are not offered.

title: Botanical Garden Seasonal Exhibit
transcript: Hello, and welcome to the university botanical garden. I'd like to tell you about a special exhibit we've just opened that I think you'll really enjoy. For the next three months, our conservatory is hosting a display of carnivorous plants, the kind that actually trap and digest insects. Now, I know that sounds like something out of a science fiction movie, but these are real plants that have evolved remarkable ways to survive in poor, boggy soils where nutrients are scarce. The star of the show is the Venus flytrap, whose hinged leaves snap shut in a fraction of a second when an insect touches the tiny trigger hairs inside. We also have pitcher plants, which lure insects into slippery, tube-shaped leaves filled with digestive fluid. A quick word of caution: please don't trigger the traps yourself by poking them. Each trap can only close a limited number of times before it wears out and dies, so touching them actually harms the plant. Our staff will do live feeding demonstrations twice a day, at eleven and at three, and that's the best time to see the traps in action. The exhibit is included with your regular admission, no extra ticket needed. And if you'd like to take one home, the garden shop sells young flytraps along with a care guide, since they do need a bit of special attention. Enjoy your visit, and feel free to ask our gardeners anything.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the announcement?
options: To warn visitors that the conservatory is closing
To introduce a special exhibit of carnivorous plants
To explain how to become a garden volunteer
To advertise a sale on gardening tools
questions.explanation: The speaker introduces the new carnivorous-plant exhibit and its features; the other options are not the focus.

questions.q: According to the speaker, why have these plants evolved to trap insects?
options: To defend themselves from larger animals
To attract pollinators to their flowers
To obtain nutrients missing from poor soils
To store water during dry seasons
questions.explanation: The speaker says they survive in nutrient-poor boggy soils by trapping insects; the other explanations are not given.

questions.q: Why does the speaker ask visitors not to trigger the traps?
options: Because the traps can pinch a person's finger
Because each trap wears out after closing a limited number of times
Because the plants are poisonous to touch
Because it disturbs the feeding demonstrations
questions.explanation: The speaker explains traps can only close a limited number of times before dying, so poking harms the plant.

questions.q: What can be inferred about the flytraps sold in the garden shop?
options: They require some special care to keep alive
They are fully grown adult plants
They cannot survive outside the conservatory
They are given away for free with admission
questions.explanation: The shop sells young flytraps with a care guide because they need special attention; the other claims are unsupported.

title: Dormitory Fire Drill Notice
transcript: Good evening, residents. This is your resident advisor with an important notice about tomorrow. At ten o'clock in the morning, we'll be conducting a scheduled fire drill for the entire building. I'm telling you now so nobody's caught off guard when the alarm goes off. When you hear it, please treat it exactly as you would a real emergency. Stop what you're doing, grab your keys, and leave the building immediately using the nearest stairwell. Do not, under any circumstances, use the elevators. In a real fire, elevators can trap you or open onto a smoke-filled floor, which is why we always take the stairs. Once you're outside, gather at the designated assembly point, which is the grass field across from the parking lot, not the front sidewalk. We need everyone in that field so the floor captains can count heads and confirm the building is empty. Please stay there until you hear the all-clear announcement. The whole thing should take no more than fifteen minutes. Now, I realize some of you have early classes or might be sleeping after a night shift, but participation is mandatory. It's a safety requirement, and honestly, knowing the evacuation route could save your life someday. If you use a mobility device or need any assistance evacuating, please come talk to me tonight so we can make a plan for you. Thanks, everyone, and I'll see you out on the field tomorrow.

questions.q: Why is the resident advisor giving this notice?
options: To announce a real fire in the building
To inform residents about a scheduled fire drill
To report a change in dormitory rules
To recruit volunteers to be floor captains
questions.explanation: The advisor is notifying residents about tomorrow's planned drill; there is no real fire or rule change.

questions.q: Why does the speaker tell residents not to use the elevators?
options: Because the elevators are out of service
Because using them wastes electricity
Because elevators can trap people or open onto smoke
Because the stairwell is faster to reach the field
questions.explanation: The speaker explains elevators can trap you or open onto a smoke-filled floor in a real fire; the other reasons are not stated.

questions.q: Where are residents supposed to gather during the drill?
options: On the front sidewalk of the building
In the lobby near the front desk
In the parking lot
On the grass field across from the parking lot
questions.explanation: The speaker specifies the grass field across from the parking lot, explicitly not the front sidewalk.

questions.q: What does the speaker ask residents who need evacuation assistance to do?
options: Speak with the advisor tonight to make a plan
Stay in their rooms during the drill
Wait for the fire department to arrive
Use the elevator instead of the stairs
questions.explanation: The advisor asks those needing assistance to talk to him tonight to plan; the other options contradict the safety guidance.

title: Bookstore Textbook Buyback Program
transcript: Hey, everybody, quick announcement from the campus bookstore before the semester wraps up. Starting next week, we're running our end-of-term textbook buyback program, and this is your chance to turn those heavy books you're done with into some cash. Here's how it works. Bring your used textbooks to the counter near the main entrance between Monday and Friday, nine to five. We'll scan the barcode, check the condition, and make you an offer on the spot. Now, I want to be upfront about a few things so nobody's disappointed. First, we can only buy back books that will be used again in courses next term, so if a professor is switching editions or dropping a title, unfortunately we can't take it. Second, condition matters a lot. Books with heavy highlighting, water damage, or missing pages will get a lower offer, or we may not be able to accept them at all. So if your book is in decent shape, you'll get more for it. One more thing worth knowing: the earlier you sell, the better. We work with a limited budget, and once we've bought enough copies of a particular title, we stop taking that book, even if it's still in demand. So don't wait until the last day. If your book doesn't qualify here, consider selling it directly to another student online. Thanks, and good luck with finals.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the announcement?
options: A sale on new textbooks for next semester
A program to buy back used textbooks from students
A change in the bookstore's operating hours
A new online platform for students to trade books
questions.explanation: The announcement is about the buyback program for used textbooks; the other options are minor or not the focus.

questions.q: Why might the bookstore refuse to buy a particular textbook?
options: Because it will not be used in courses next term
Because it was purchased from another store
Because the student has already graduated
Because it is a hardcover rather than paperback
questions.explanation: The speaker says they only buy books used again next term; format and place of purchase are not mentioned as factors.

questions.q: Why does the speaker advise students to sell their books early?
options: Because prices drop each day of the week
Because the bookstore closes early on Fridays
Because the store stops buying a title once it has enough copies
Because early sellers receive a free gift
questions.explanation: The speaker explains the limited budget means they stop buying a title after enough copies; the other reasons are invented.

questions.q: What does the speaker suggest for books that do not qualify?
options: Donating them to the library
Returning them to the professor
Throwing them away to save space
Selling them directly to another student online
questions.explanation: The speaker recommends selling unqualified books directly to another student online; the other suggestions are not mentioned.

title: Community Garden Volunteer Day
transcript: Hi, folks, and thanks for stopping by the information table. I'm here to tell you about our community garden's big volunteer day coming up this Saturday morning. The garden, as some of you know, sits on the empty lot behind the community center, and over the past two years, neighborhood volunteers have transformed it from a weedy patch into a real source of fresh vegetables for local families. This Saturday, from eight in the morning until noon, we're getting together to prepare the beds for the fall planting season. No gardening experience is needed at all, I promise. We'll have experienced gardeners on hand to show you everything, from turning the soil to spacing out the seedlings. Just wear clothes you don't mind getting dirty, and bring a water bottle and maybe a hat, because there's not much shade out there. We'll provide all the tools, gloves, and even a light breakfast of coffee and pastries to keep everyone fueled. Here's the part I really want to emphasize: everything we grow gets donated to the community food pantry down the street, which serves hundreds of families every month. So the few hours you spend digging in the dirt really do go toward feeding your neighbors. If you can't make it Saturday but still want to help, we're always looking for people to water the beds on weekday evenings. Sign up right here at the table, and I hope to see you out in the garden.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To sell vegetables grown in the community garden
To invite people to a garden volunteer day
To announce the closing of the community center
To teach an advanced gardening course
questions.explanation: The speaker is recruiting volunteers for Saturday's garden workday; the other options misstate the purpose.

questions.q: What does the speaker emphasize about the vegetables grown in the garden?
options: They are sold to raise money for the garden
They are shared only among the volunteers
They are donated to a local food pantry
They are shipped to other cities
questions.explanation: The speaker stresses that everything grown is donated to the community food pantry; the other uses are contradicted.

questions.q: What does the speaker suggest volunteers bring on Saturday?
options: Their own gardening tools and gloves
A water bottle and a hat
Seeds and fertilizer for the beds
A packed lunch and a folding chair
questions.explanation: The speaker asks volunteers to bring a water bottle and hat, noting tools, gloves, and breakfast are provided.

questions.q: What can people do if they cannot attend on Saturday?
options: Help water the beds on weekday evenings
Donate money instead of time
Attend a makeup session next month
Take home free vegetables from the garden
questions.explanation: The speaker offers weekday-evening watering as an alternative; the other options are not mentioned.
